summary: nolimit-x ships as fully obfuscated bulk SMS/SMTP sender ('red-team' tool)
details: Package self-describes as an 'Advanced email sender' with keywords including 'red-team' and 'smtp'. The entire runtime tree under.ad/ (45 files including the main/bin entry x0.js, plus sms-providers, phone-validator, smtp-health-cache, web-command, from-generator) is shipped as obfuscator.io-packed JavaScript with rotated string arrays and an RC4-style decoder, produced by a build step (scripts/obfuscate.js) using the javascript-obfuscator devDependency. The CLI implements bulk SMS delivery via carrier email-to-SMS gateways (sendViaSmtpGateway loops over verizon/att/tmobile/sprint), bulk email via SMTP/OAuth, contact harvesting via Microsoft Graph and IMAP, open-redirect scanning, and DKIM key generation. main and bin both point at.ad/x0.js, which ends in program.parse(); a consumer that require()s the package will trigger commander argv parsing but no destructive or exfiltration behavior — the spam/abuse functions are gated behind user-typed subcommands. There is no install-time lifecycle script, no outbound network call on import or install, and no evidence the obfuscated code targets the installer's environment, credentials, or filesystem. The package is dual-use spam/abuse infrastructure that operates against third parties (SMS/SMTP recipients, carrier gateways, email providers) only when an operator deliberately runs the CLI with their own configured credentials. Routing to human review for policy decision: while not a supply-chain attack against installers, the combination of self-declared bulk-sender purpose plus full-tree obfuscation (whose only effect is to defeat registry-side review) is the shape registries typically remove under abuse policy.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
